content.full_text:
Flrn Porrrrcar PnacrrcBs CouurssroN
P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street . Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
February 8, 2001
Honorable LauraHammes
San Diego Superior Court
P.O.Box2724
San Diego, CA 92112
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-022
Dear Judge Hammes:
This is in response to your request for advice under the statement of economic
interest disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.l
QUESTION
As a beneficiary of your parents' living trust, must you disclose the assets held by
that trust?
CONCLUSION
No. Generally, officials with a beneficial interest in an irrevocable trust, must
disclose the assets of that trust unless the trustee has unlimited powers to invade the trust
principal. Based on the information provided, this appears to be the case under your fact
pattern.
FACTS
Your parents established a living trust in which each spouse left his/her half of the
community assets and all of his/her separate assets to you and your brother, to be held in
trust by the surviving spouse. The surviving spouse has full control over the deceased
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commissionregulations appearat Tltle2,sections 18109-
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.
aql@r


,!
File No. A-01-022
Page2
spouse's estate for the reminder of his/her life, and may buy or sell, and even deplete the
estate if necessary.
Your father is deceased and your mother controls his half of the estate, as well as her
own.
ANALYSIS
You are preparing your annual statement of economic interests. As a judge, you
are required to disclose certain sources of income, investments and interests in real
property. You are asking what is your responsibility to disclose stocks held through your
parents' living trust for which you Ne a beneficiary.
The term "investment" means any financial interest in or security issued by a
business entity, including, but not limited to, common stock, preferred stock, rights,
warrants, options, debt instruments and any parhrership or ownership interest.
Investments of an individual include a pro rata share of investments of any business
entity, mutual fund, or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section s2034.)
Regulation 18234 sets forth the requirements for reporting interests in trusts. It
provides, in pertinent part:
"(c) For purposes of determining whether an official has an economic interest
in interests in real property, sources of income, and investments of a trust, the
official has a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest in a trust if the official is:
{.***
(2) Abeneficiary and:
(A) Presently receives income; or
(B) Has an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal. For
purposes of this subsection, an individual has an irrevocable future right to
receive income or principal if the trust is irrevocable, unless one of the following
applies:
(i) 
Powers exist to consume, invade, or appoint the principal for the
benefit of beneficiaries other than the official and such powers are not limited by
an ascertainable standard relating to the health, education, support, or
maintenance of the beneficiaries; or
(iD Under the terms of the trust, someone other than the official can
designate the persons who shall possess or enjoy the trust property or income."


t
File No. A-01-022
Page 3
Applying the regulation, an official does not have an economic interest in the
assets of an irrevocable trust if unlimited powers exist to invade the trust principal for the
benefit of someone other than the official, or someone other than the official can change
the beneficiaries of the trust. (Van de Kamp Advice Letter, No. A-88-169.) In either
circumstance, the official might not receive his or her share of the trust principal or
income due to events beyond his or her control. (Boitano Advice Letter, No. A-97-
ss7(a).)
According to your letter, your mother has full control over your father's estate for
the remainder of her life and may buy or sell, or totally deplete the assets of the trust. It
appears from the statements in your letter that while you have an interest in an
irrevocable trust, your mother has unlimited powers with respect to the assets of the
trust."
Applyrng the regulation and the Van de Kamp and, Boitano letters to the facts you
provided, you do not have an economic interest in the assets of your parents' trust. You
do not need to ascertain the assets of the trust for purposes of completing your statement
of economic interest.
I trust this answers your question sufficiently. If you have any further questions,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division
LM/IETtjt
' This letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in
providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FppC Ops. 71.)
B

pattern.
